examples.title: Algorithm: Base and Limit Register Check
examples.description: Checks if a logical address is within the valid range defined by the base and limit registers.
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BaseLimitCheck
INPUT: logicalAddress, baseRegister, limitRegister
OUTPUT: valid (true/false)

BEGIN
 IF logicalAddress < 0 OR logicalAddress >= limitRegister THEN
 valid = false
 ELSE
 physicalAddress = baseRegister + logicalAddress
 IF physicalAddress < baseRegister OR physicalAddress >= baseRegister + limitRegister THEN
 valid = false
 ELSE
 valid = true
 RETURN valid
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode algorithm checks if a logical address is within the valid range defined by the base and limit registers. It first checks if the logical address is negative or greater than or equal to the limit register. If so, it returns false. Otherwise, it calculates the physical address by adding the base register to the logical address and checks if it's within the valid range. If so, it returns true; otherwise, it returns false.

examples.title: Python: First-Fit Algorithm
examples.description: Implements the first-fit algorithm for memory allocation.
examples.explanation: This Python code implements the first-fit algorithm for memory allocation. It takes a list of memory blocks and a process size as input and returns the index of the first block that can accommodate the process. If no suitable block is found, it returns -1.

examples.title: C: Next-Fit Algorithm
examples.description: Implements the next-fit algorithm for memory allocation.
examples.explanation: This C code implements the next-fit algorithm for memory allocation. It takes an array of memory blocks, the number of blocks, a process size, and a pointer to the last allocated block index as input. It returns the index of the next block that can accommodate the process, wrapping around to the beginning of the array if necessary. If no suitable block is found, it returns -1.
